metadata.author: John of Damascus: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith

ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning what is Voluntary and what is Involuntary.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The voluntary implies a certain definite action, and so-called involuntariness also implies a certain definite action.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further, many attribute true involuntariness not only to suffering, but even to action.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: We must then understand action to be rational energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Actions are followed by praise or blame, and some of them are accompanied with pleasure and others with pain; some are to be desired by the actor, others are to be shunned: further, of those that are desirable, some are always so, others only at some particular time.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And so it is also with those that are to be shunned.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, some actions enlist pity and are pardonable, others are hateful and deserve punishment.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Voluntariness, then, is assuredly followed by praise or blame, and renders the action pleasurable and desirable to the actor, either for all time or for the moment of its performance.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Involuntariness, on the other hand, brings merited pity or pardon in its train, and renders the act painful and undesirable to the doer, and makes him leave it in a state of incompleteness even though force is brought to bear upon him.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further, what is involuntary, depends in part on force and in part on ignorance.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It depends on force when the creative beginning in cause is from without, that is to say, when one is forced by another without being at all persuaded, or when one does not contribute to the act on one’s own impulse, or does not co-operate at all, or do on one’s own account that which is exacted by force.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Thus we may give this definition: “An involuntary act is one in which the beginning is from without, and where one does not contribute at all on one’s own impulse to that which one is forced.”

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And by beginning we mean the creative cause.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: All involuntary act depends, on the other hand, on ignorance, when one is not the cause of the ignorance one’s self, but events just so happen.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For, if one commits murder while drunk, it is an act of ignorance, but yet not involuntary: for one was one’s self responsible for the cause of the ignorance, that is to say, the drunkenness.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if while shooting at the customary range one slew one’s father who happened to be passing by, this would be termed an ignorant and involuntary act.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: As, then, that which is involuntary is in two parts, one depending on force, the other on ignorance, that which is voluntary is the opposite of both.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For that which is voluntary is the result neither of force nor of ignorance.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: A voluntary act, then, is one of which the beginning or cause originates in an actor, who knows each individual circumstance through which and in which the action takes place.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: By “individual” is meant what the rhetoricians call circumstantial elements: for instance, the actor, the sufferer, the action (perchance a murder), the instrument, the place, the time, the manner, the reason of the action.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Notice that there are certain things that occupy a place intermediate between what is voluntary and what is involuntary.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Although they are unpleasant and painful we welcome them as the escape from a still greater trouble; for instance, to escape shipwreck we cast the cargo overboard.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Notice also that children and irrational creatures perform voluntary actions, but these do not involve the exercise of choice: further, all our actions that are done in anger and without previous deliberation are voluntary actions, but do not in the least involve free choice.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Also, if a friend suddenly appears on the scene, or if one unexpectedly lights on a treasure, so far as we are concerned it is quite voluntary, but there is no question of choice in the matter.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For all these things are voluntary, because we desire pleasure from them, but they do not by any means imply choice, because they are not the result of deliberation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And deliberation must assuredly precede choice, as we have said above.
